design_philosophy.core_loop: Inhale enemies, gain copy abilities, use powers to navigate levels and defeat bosses, recover Star Rod pieces
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource management of copy abilities (limited uses, loss on hit) vs exploration and combat flexibility
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Cute, adaptable hero gaining diverse powers to overcome varied challenges across Dream Land
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Copy ability system as core mechanic, not optional—designed to address predecessor's simplicity criticism. Forces replaying levels with different abilities for full access. Auto-saves after each level, reducing punishment. Introduces graphical sophistication on aging hardware (late NES lifecycle) with Kirby depicted in color for first time.

difficulty.lives_system: Standard lives system; losing all health or falling off screen costs a life; game over on life loss
difficulty.checkpointing: Auto-save after each level completion; no mid-level checkpoints
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Minigames available in most worlds to gain extra lives; ability to discard/swap copy abilities at will; health recovery via food items and temporary invulnerability pickups; optional boss arenas for ability acquisition

progression.upgrade_system: Copy abilities acquired by inhaling and swallowing specific enemies; abilities provide new movement options (UFO flight, Wheel speed, Fire projectiles, Sword attacks, etc.) and access gated areas; limited-use abilities deplete on usage
progression.unlock_mechanism: Seven themed worlds with 4-5 levels each; boss doors at end of each world; Warp Star doors allow backtracking to previous worlds; museum rooms provide guaranteed ability access; miniboss arenas grant ability copies on defeat
replayability_hooks: Multiple copy abilities change how same level plays
Hidden areas accessible only with specific powers
Minigames offer extra lives and encourages replays
Ability roulette system when swallowing multiple enemies creates variability
Museum rooms require revisiting worlds to obtain all powers

world_building_techniques: Themed worlds (Vegetable Valley, etc.) with visual storytelling
Opening narrative framing: Nightmare corrupts Fountain of Dreams, King Dedede steals Star Rod and distributes pieces to minions
Bosses tied to world themes; miniboss encounters reinforce world identity
Final sequence: Nightmare flees to outer space after Dedede defeated; climactic boss fight with Nightmare

boss_design.philosophy: Each world culminates in signature boss reflecting world theme; minibosses serve as intermediate challenges granting copy abilities; bosses designed to be learnable through pattern recognition rather than twitch reflex
notable_bosses: Poppy Bros. Sr. (miniboss with unused animation frames)
Mr. Frosty (miniboss with conditional fall animation)
Rolling Turtle (miniboss with unused shell-hide frame)

faction_indicators: King Dedede as misguided ally (unknowingly helps stop Nightmare by taking Star Rod)
Dedede's minions as distributed guardians holding Star Rod pieces
Nightmare as external corrupting force uniting all Dream Land inhabitants against common threat
danger_signals: Fountain of Dreams corrupted—ecosystem health threatened
Nightmare spreading bad dreams suggests existential threat to consciousness/rest
King Dedede's thievery initially misread as villainy before true antagonist revealed
environmental_storytelling: Themed worlds (Vegetable Valley, Butter Building, Mushroom Kingdom-adjacent settings) suggest diverse Dream Land ecosystem
Fountain of Dreams as central location and narrative anchor
Star Rod pieces scattered across worlds suggest Dedede deliberately distributed them for safekeeping
Unused dark room tileset suggests cut areas that would have expanded world scope
